tasks.prompt:
Warm-up: Compare the two deploy approaches from the lecture. For each of the following statements, mark it as applying to Approach A ('kubectl apply -f'), Approach B ('kubectl set image'), both, or neither — and give a one-line justification.

(i) The file in Git always reflects exactly what is running in the cluster.
(ii) Useful when you want to change only the image tag without modifying any other manifest field.
(iii) Triggers a rolling update on the cluster.
(iv) The cluster state can silently drift from what is committed in Git.
(v) Recommended as the long-term approach because it supports GitOps workflows.
tasks.starter:
# Fill in: A / B / Both / Neither — and one-line justification.

# (i) Git file always reflects cluster state
# Answer: ________
# Justification: ________

# (ii) Update only image tag, no file edit needed
# Answer: ________
# Justification: ________

# (iii) Triggers a rolling update
# Answer: ________
# Justification: ________

# (iv) Cluster state can drift from Git
# Answer: ________
# Justification: ________

# (v) Recommended for GitOps / long-term
# Answer: ________
# Justification: ________
tasks.solution:
# (i) Git file always reflects cluster state
# Answer: A
# Justification: Approach A applies the manifest file, so the file is always the
#                applied state; Approach B mutates the cluster in-place without
#                touching the file, so the file can be stale.

# (ii) Update only image tag, no file edit needed
# Answer: B
# Justification: 'kubectl set image' targets a single container's image field directly;
#                Approach A requires you to first update the tag in the YAML file.

# (iii) Triggers a rolling update
# Answer: Both
# Justification: Any change to the Deployment's Pod template (image or otherwise)
#                triggers a rolling update regardless of which kubectl command caused it.

# (iv) Cluster state can drift from Git
# Answer: B
# Justification: 'kubectl set image' updates the live Deployment but leaves the
#                manifest file unchanged, creating a gap between Git and cluster reality.

# (v) Recommended for GitOps / long-term
# Answer: A
# Justification: Applying the manifest file keeps Git as the single source of truth,
#                which is the prerequisite for GitOps tooling (Argo CD, Flux).
tasks.explanation: Both approaches trigger a rolling update because they both modify the Deployment's Pod template spec. The real difference is about consistency: Approach A (apply -f) makes the file the authoritative state, so the cluster always matches your repository. Approach B (set image) is a quick in-cluster mutation that leaves the repository out of date. For day-to-day use in a pipeline that only updates the image tag, either works; for operational maturity and GitOps readiness, Approach A is the correct long-term choice.
hints: A 'kubectl apply' with no changes to the YAML is a no-op — Kubernetes checks the new desired state against the current state and does nothing if they match.
You can see the last-applied configuration with: kubectl get deployment my-app -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations.kubectl\.kubernetes\.io/last-applied-configuration}'

In GitOps, the pipeline never runs kubectl directly — it commits the updated manifest to Git and lets a controller (Argo CD/Flux) apply it. Approach A is the necessary stepping stone to reach that model.

tasks.prompt: Core practice A — Write the complete deploy stage for the Jenkinsfile. It should: (1) authenticate to EKS using 'aws eks update-kubeconfig', (2) update the image in the running Deployment using 'kubectl set image', and (3) wait for the rollout to complete with a 120-second timeout. Use the environment variables already defined in the pipeline: AWS_REGION, CLUSTER, REGISTRY, ECR_REPO, IMAGE_TAG. The Deployment is named 'my-app' and the container is also named 'my-app'.
tasks.explanation: 'aws eks update-kubeconfig' must run first — without it, kubectl has no cluster to talk to. 'kubectl set image deployment/my-app my-app=<image>' updates the image of the container named 'my-app' inside the Deployment named 'my-app'. The format is: deployment/<deployment-name> <container-name>=<full-image-reference>. The container name must exactly match the 'name:' field in the Pod template's containers list. Finally, 'kubectl rollout status --timeout=120s' blocks the pipeline until all Pods in the Deployment are healthy under the new image, or exits non-zero if the timeout is exceeded — turning a stuck rollout into a pipeline failure you can act on.
hints: If IMAGE_TAG is the commit SHA (e.g., 'a1b9f3c'), the full image reference is: 111122223333.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-app:a1b9f3c — match this format in REGISTRY/ECR_REPO:IMAGE_TAG.
The CLUSTER environment variable must be added to the Jenkinsfile environment block: CLUSTER = 'my-cluster'
Test with a dry run first: kubectl set image deployment/my-app my-app=<image> --dry-run=client — this shows the change without applying it.

tasks.prompt: Core practice B — Write the alternative deploy stage using Approach A (manifest rewrite + kubectl apply). The deployment.yaml file contains 'image: IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER' as a placeholder. Use 'sed' to substitute the real image reference in-place, then apply both deployment.yaml and service.yaml, then wait for the rollout.
tasks.explanation: 'sed -i' edits the file in place. The substitution pattern 's|IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER|<real-image>|' uses '|' as the delimiter instead of '/' because the image URL contains forward slashes — using '/' as a delimiter would require escaping every slash in the URL, making the command unreadable. After the substitution, 'deployment.yaml' contains the exact ECR image reference for this build, and 'kubectl apply -f' applies the complete, current state of the manifest to the cluster. This is the approach that keeps the manifest as the single source of truth: what is in Git (after the pipeline runs) exactly matches what EKS runs.
hints: In deployment.yaml, the placeholder line should look like: image: IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER — the sed command replaces that entire token.
On macOS, 'sed -i' requires an empty string argument: sed -i '' 's|...|...|' file. On Linux (and Jenkins agents running Linux) the empty string is not needed.

If the Jenkinsfile runs in a Docker agent that does not have 'sed', you can use Python: python3 -c "import re,sys; open('deployment.yaml','w').write(re.sub('IMAGE_PLACEHOLDER','${REGISTRY}/${ECR_REPO}:${IMAGE_TAG}',open('deployment.yaml').read()))"

tasks.prompt: Applied challenge — You deployed image tag 'abc1234' and 'kubectl rollout status' exited with a non-zero code (timeout). Investigate the failed rollout and recover the cluster to serve users. Write all kubectl commands needed to: (1) check the rollout status and which Pods are failing, (2) inspect the failing Pod's events to find the root cause, and (3) roll back to the previous working version.
tasks.starter:
# Step 1: See the rollout status and which Pods are stuck
kubectl rollout status deployment/my-app
kubectl get pods

# Step 2: Inspect a failing Pod
# Pick a Pod name from the output above (e.g., my-app-abc123-xyz)
kubectl describe pod ________      # look at the Events section
kubectl logs ________              # view container logs if the Pod started but crashed

# Step 3: Roll back to the previous revision
kubectl ________
tasks.solution:
# Step 1: See the rollout status and which Pods are stuck
kubectl rollout status deployment/my-app
# (This will print: 'Waiting for deployment... 1 out of 3 new replicas have been updated')
kubectl get pods
# (Look for Pods in CrashLoopBackOff, ImagePullBackOff, or Pending state)

# Step 2: Inspect a failing Pod
kubectl describe pod my-app-<new-replicaset-hash>-<pod-hash>
# Look at 'Events' at the bottom — common messages:
#   ImagePullBackOff: the image tag does not exist in ECR
#   CrashLoopBackOff: the container starts then immediately crashes
#   OOMKilled: container exceeded memory limit
kubectl logs my-app-<new-replicaset-hash>-<pod-hash>
# (Read the container's stdout/stderr for application errors)

# Step 3: Roll back to the previous revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/my-app
# Verify the rollback is in progress:
kubectl rollout status deployment/my-app
# Confirm old image is now running:
kubectl get deployment my-app -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
tasks.explanation: 'kubectl get pods' quickly shows which Pods are in a bad state (CrashLoopBackOff, ImagePullBackOff, Pending). 'kubectl describe pod <name>' shows the Event log, which is the most informative first step: ImagePullBackOff means the image tag 'abc1234' was never pushed to ECR or the tag is wrong; CrashLoopBackOff means the image pulled but the process crashes immediately (check logs). 'kubectl rollout undo' is the recovery command: it instructs the Deployment to revert to the previous ReplicaSet revision. Because the old Pods were never fully retired during the failed rollout (maxUnavailable keeps some healthy Pods alive), the rollback restores full capacity quickly. After rolling back, investigate the root cause before attempting another deploy.
hints: 'kubectl rollout history deployment/my-app' lists all revisions. Use '--to-revision=<N>' to roll back to a specific known-good version: kubectl rollout undo deployment/my-app --to-revision=2

ImagePullBackOff is always either: (a) the tag does not exist in ECR, (b) the cluster nodes lack pull permission to ECR, or (c) the registry URL is wrong. Check 'kubectl describe pod' Events for the exact error string.
After rollback, run 'kubectl get pods' and confirm all Pods return to Running state before closing the incident.
